sourceTitle: The best way to set up your team’s sidebar for clear organization

questions.question: Which keyboard shortcut toggles the Notion sidebar on Mac?

options.explanation: On macOS, pressing Command + \ opens or hides the sidebar. This is stated in the article’s “Your company’s mission control” section.

options.text: ⌘ + Shift + S
options.explanation: This shortcut does not relate to the sidebar; it’s not mentioned in the article.

options.text: ⌥ + \ (Option + Backslash)
options.explanation: Option + \ is not the shortcut for toggling the sidebar.

options.text: ⌃ + \ (Control + Backslash)
options.explanation: Control + \ is the Windows shortcut, not the Mac shortcut.

questions.question: What is the new name for the former “Updates” menu in the sidebar?

options.text: Notifications
options.explanation: The article does not rename “Updates” to Notifications.

options.text: Inbox
options.explanation: The article explains that “Updates” was changed to “Inbox,” offering a better look and workflow.

options.text: Activity Feed
options.explanation: Activity Feed is not mentioned as a rename for Updates.

options.text: Alerts
options.explanation: Alerts is not the new name for Updates.

questions.question: Which sidebar section shows pages that are shared with at least one other teammate but not with the whole workspace?

options.text: Workspace
options.explanation: Workspace holds top‑level pages accessible to all members, not just a subset.

options.text: Shared
options.explanation: The “Shared” section contains pages that are shared with one or more teammates, as described in the article.

options.text: Private
options.explanation: Private pages are only visible to you until you explicitly share them.

options.text: Favorites
options.explanation: Favorites is a shortcut to any page you’ve marked, regardless of its sharing status.

questions.question: How can you create a new page directly from the sidebar?

options.text: Right‑click any existing page and select “New Sub‑page.”
options.explanation: While possible, the article emphasizes using the “+” button next to Workspace, Shared, or Private.

options.text: Click the “+” next to Workspace, Shared, or Private, depending on where the page belongs.
options.explanation: The article states that a plus sign next to those sections lets you add a new page in that area.

options.text: Press ⌘ + N while the sidebar is focused.
options.explanation: The article does not mention this shortcut for creating pages.

options.text: Drag an existing page onto the sidebar background.
options.explanation: Dragging moves pages; it does not create new ones.

questions.question: According to the guide, what is the recommended strategy for top‑level pages in the sidebar?

options.text: Create a top‑level page for every individual document to keep everything visible at a glance.
options.explanation: The guide warns against overloading the sidebar with too many top‑level pages.

options.text: Limit the number of top‑level pages, using each as a department or company‑wide hub, and nest sub‑pages underneath.
options.explanation: The article advises keeping a small number of high‑level pages (departments, company‑wide hubs) and using toggles for sub‑pages.

options.text: Put all pages—private, shared, and workspace—in the top‑level area to avoid using toggles.
options.explanation: Mixing all pages at the top level defeats the purpose of organized nesting.

options.text: Duplicate each top‑level page for every team to avoid permission issues.
options.explanation: Duplicating pages is unnecessary and not suggested in the guide.

questions.question: Where do you go to browse Notion’s library of pre‑made templates?

options.text: Click the “Templates” button in the sidebar.
options.explanation: The article explains that opening “Templates” in the sidebar opens the template gallery.

options.text: Open the Settings menu and select “Template Library.”
options.explanation: Templates are not accessed through Settings.

options.text: Press ⌘ + T to open the template picker.
options.explanation: No shortcut for templates is mentioned.

options.text: Visit the Notion website and download templates as zip files.
options.explanation: Templates are integrated inside the app via the sidebar, not downloaded as zip files.

questions.question: If you have a page in the Private section that you now want teammates to see, what is the correct sequence of actions?

options.text: Add teammates in the sharing settings, then drag the page to the Shared section.
options.explanation: The guide says you add collaborators via the sharing menu, after which the page moves to Shared.

options.text: Rename the page with a “shared‑” prefix; it automatically appears in Shared.
options.explanation: Renaming does not affect sharing status.

options.text: Copy the page content into a new page inside Workspace; the original stays Private.
options.explanation: While possible, the article recommends sharing the original page rather than copying.

options.text: Delete the Private page and recreate it directly in the Shared section.
options.explanation: Deleting loses history; the recommended method is to share and drag.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the Quick Find feature in the sidebar?

options.text: To locate and open any page, including searching inside page content.
options.explanation: Quick Find lets you search for anything in the workspace, even words inside pages, as described.

options.text: To filter the list of favorite pages.
options.explanation: Favorites have their own section; Quick Find is a broader search tool.

options.text: To change the sidebar’s color theme.
options.explanation: Sidebar theming is not a function of Quick Find.

options.text: To toggle the sidebar between locked and hidden states.
options.explanation: Locking/hiding uses arrows or shortcuts, not Quick Find.

questions.question: Which of the following is a recommended way to keep old projects accessible without cluttering the main view?

options.text: Create an “Archive” top‑level page and drag finished projects there.
options.explanation: The guide suggests an archive page for old projects to keep the main view tidy.

options.text: Leave them in the Workspace section but rename them with “ARCHIVE_” prefix.
options.explanation: Renaming alone does not hide them; they still occupy top‑level space.

options.text: Move them to the Private section permanently.
options.explanation: Private is intended for work not yet ready to share, not for archival.

options.text: Delete them and rely on the Trash to retrieve if needed.
options.explanation: Deleting removes them from active view and may be permanent; not recommended for easy reference.

questions.question: Which external tools can you import data from using the Import button in the sidebar?

options.text: Only Evernote and Asana.
options.explanation: The article lists more than just Evernote and Asana.

options.text: Evernote, Asana, Confluence, and other supported software.
options.explanation: The article explicitly mentions importing from Evernote, Asana, Confluence, and other software.

options.text: Google Docs, Microsoft Word, and PDFs.
options.explanation: These formats are not listed as import sources in the guide.

options.text: Only CSV files from spreadsheets.
options.explanation: CSV import is not highlighted; the guide focuses on other productivity tools.
